process_and_outcome: Microsoft has maintained compliance through annual SOC 2 audits, ISO 27001 certifications, and no major enforcement actions related to data misuse. Customers successfully defend in regulatory inquiries by leveraging Microsoft's processor status.

example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, used its mobile app and website to collect personal information including purchase history, browsing behavior, and precise location data via SDKs from partners like LiveRamp and Criteo. This data was shared with third parties for cross-site tracking and targeted advertising without providing CCPA-required opt-out links or 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' notices. The disclosures occurred for 'monetization' purposes, involving valuable consideration, from 2018 onward affecting California consumers.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Sephora settled with the California AG for $1.2 million, the first CCPA enforcement action. Sephora agreed to update privacy notices, implement opt-out mechanisms, and limit data sharing to service providers.

example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) structures its service agreements to act strictly as a data processor for customer data hosted on EC2, S3, and other services. The AWS Data Processing Addendum (DPA) mandates that customer data not be used for AWS product improvement, advertising, or sold/shared without authorization. Features include data portability, encryption keys under customer control, and subprocessors requiring customer approval. AWS publishes compliance reports showing no data monetization outside customer instructions.
process_and_outcome: AWS undergoes regular third-party audits (SOC 1/2/3, ISO 27001) with clean findings. No enforcement actions for breaching processor role; supports customer GDPR/CCPA compliance defenses.

example_name: FTC v. GoodRx Data Sharing Enforcement
example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount platform, shared user health data including prescription records, names, and IP addresses with Google, Facebook, and other advertising platforms via tracking pixels from 2017 to 2020. This enabled targeted ads based on sensitive health information, constituting disclosures for valuable consideration without user knowledge or HIPAA/CCPA-compliant notices. Data was not limited to service fulfillment but used for behavioral advertising across the internet.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the FTC banned GoodRx from sharing health data for ads, required deletion of shared data, and mandated a privacy program with biennial audits. No monetary fine, but strict injunctions applied.

example_background: Salesforce's cloud CRM platform processes customer data under agreements designating it as a processor/service provider. The Data Processing Addendum prohibits Salesforce from using customer data for training AI models, advertising, or any non-service purpose. Customers control data access, retention, and sharing, with tools for exports and deletions. Transparency includes subprocessor lists and annual certifications ensuring no data sales or shares.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce maintains compliance via ISO 27001, SOC 2/3 reports, and Trust Arc certifications. No regulatory actions for data misuse; enables customer compliance in audits.

example_background: Salesforce, a leading cloud-based CRM platform provider, processes customer data on behalf of its enterprise clients to deliver services like sales automation, customer service, and marketing tools. The platform handles personal data such as contact details, email interactions, and behavioral data uploaded by customers. Salesforce maintains strict data processing agreements, invests in ISO 27001 certified security controls, regular third-party audits, and features like Shield Platform Encryption to ensure customer data remains segregated and protected while in its custody.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce has undergone numerous SOC 2 Type II audits and GDPR compliance certifications without major enforcement actions. It demonstrates ongoing adherence through transparency reports and trust centers, avoiding significant fines or breaches attributed to its processing practices.

example_name: Google Cloud Customer Data Handling under GDPR
example_background: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) acts as a data processor for enterprise customers worldwide, handling personal data in services like BigQuery, Compute Engine, and AI/ML tools. Following GDPR implementation in 2018, Google updated its Data Processing Addendum (DPA) to explicitly define customer data, ensure sub-processor transparency, and provide tools for data residency and deletion. GCP maintains EU Model Clauses compliance and invests in data centers across regions to control data location as per customer instructions.
process_and_outcome: Google faced scrutiny but resolved early GDPR concerns through DPA revisions and DPA certifications. No major fines related to customer data processing; instead, it received approvals for data transfers and continues to publish compliance reports.

example_name: Microsoft Azure Compliance with Data Processing Agreements
example_background: Microsoft Azure provides cloud infrastructure services where it processes customer-uploaded personal data for computing, storage, and analytics. Azure's Data Protection Addendum clearly delineates customer data from Microsoft's operational data, offering features like customer-managed keys, data locality controls, and compliance with 100+ certifications. Customers retain ownership and control over data configuration and deletion.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft maintains compliance through annual audits and has not faced enforcement actions for mishandling customer data in processing roles. It publishes detailed trust documentation and supports customer-led compliance programs.

example_background: Automatic Data Processing (ADP), a payroll and HR services provider, experienced unauthorized access to client data portals affecting thousands of companies. Attackers used stolen credentials to access personal data like Social Security numbers and wage information processed by ADP on behalf of its business customers for payroll services.
process_and_outcome: ADP notified affected parties and enhanced multi-factor authentication. No major regulatory fines were imposed, but it led to class-action settlements totaling millions.

example_background: CMT Digital, LLC, operator of the Gannett-owned newspaper website USA Today, failed to display a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on its homepage and other key web pages as mandated by the CCPA. The site collected personal information from California consumers through cookies and pixels for advertising purposes and shared it with third parties. Despite having a privacy policy that referenced CCPA rights, the absence of the required opt-out link on prominent pages made it difficult for consumers to exercise their rights. This issue persisted across the website's high-traffic pages visited by millions of users monthly.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General announced a $1.2 million settlement in January 2023. CMT Digital was required to add the mandatory CCPA link to all relevant pages, conduct privacy assessments, and certify compliance annually to the AG for three years.

example_background: DoorDash, a food delivery platform, retained geolocation, purchase history, and other personal data of California users for longer periods than necessary after they deleted their accounts or requested deletion under CCPA. The company stored this data for business analytics and advertising purposes beyond what was required for service provision, without clear disclosure or justification. This affected millions of users whose data remained in DoorDash's systems post-termination of service.
process_and_outcome: In December 2024, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) settled with DoorDash for $9.5 million. The agreement mandated improved data deletion processes, data minimization practices, and regular compliance audits. DoorDash also committed to shorter retention periods and enhanced consumer request handling.

example_background: Vizio collected viewing histories from 11 million smart TVs via automatic content recognition software, generating inferred demographic and interest data. This data was sold to third parties without explicit user consent, combining it with other personal information for detailed consumer profiles used in advertising. The practices involved tracking viewing habits across households and sharing with data brokers.
process_and_outcome: The FTC and state AGs settled in February 2017 for $2.2 million in penalties plus injunctive relief. Vizio was required to provide clear opt-out notices, limit data sharing, delete prior data, and allow easy smart TV data controls.

example_background: The French CNIL fined Amazon France Logistiques €32 million in 2020 for excessive employee monitoring. The company used software to track delivery drivers' activities, including exact routes, stops, and package scans via smartphones. Data logs captured timestamps, GPS coordinates, and performance metrics, processed without sufficient legal basis or transparency. Employees were not informed about the extent of tracking, and data was retained longer than necessary for algorithmic management decisions.
process_and_outcome: CNIL's investigation, triggered by employee complaints and inspections, led to the fine. Amazon adjusted its monitoring practices, provided clearer privacy notices, and limited data retention. The decision set a precedent for workplace surveillance in France.

example_background: In 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) concluded an investigation into Microsoft’s EU-US data transfers for its commercial cloud services. Concerns involved use of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) supplemented by Additional Safeguards Measures (ASMs) post-Schrems II. The probe examined data processing arrangements where Microsoft acted as processor for enterprise customers, focusing on encryption, pseudonymization, and oversight of subprocessors.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued a €1.2 billion fine but suspended most pending appeals. Draft decisions included orders for compliance enhancements. Microsoft committed to advanced data protection commitments, influencing global cloud standards.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland WhatsApp GDPR Fine 2021
example_background: In 2021, the Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million for transparency breaches regarding data sharing with Meta group companies. WhatsApp's privacy policy failed to explain processing activities clearly, especially Facebook user data sharing for advertising since 2018 updates. The update led to user uproar and complaints across Europe. Processing lacked adequate legal basis under GDPR for combined datasets.
process_and_outcome: After a multi-year investigation coordinated with other EU DPAs, the fine was imposed. WhatsApp revised policies and implemented better notice mechanisms. Appeals were ongoing as of 2023.

example_background: The Irish DPC investigated Salesforce as a data processor under GDPR for EU customers using its CRM platform. The focus was on subprocessors, transfer mechanisms to the US, and contractual obligations with controllers. Salesforce provides cloud services where it processes personal data on behalf of controllers, with transparency reports detailing data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: The inquiry concluded without a fine, with the DPC finding adequate processor agreements and transfer tools compliant post-Schrems II. Salesforce published enhanced transparency reports and DPA updates, demonstrating adherence.

example_background: Data from up to 87 million Facebook users was accessed by Cambridge Analytica via a third-party app that collected profile information and data from friends without consent. The data was used for political advertising. Facebook's platform APIs allowed extensive data harvesting until changes in 2014-2015, but prior collection persisted.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled for $5 billion, requiring new oversight and privacy program. ICO fined £500,000. Facebook enhanced app review processes and data sharing controls.

example_name: Microsoft Azure Breach by Ukraine Support Group 2022
example_background: The Ukraine Support Group for Defense of Animals accidentally exposed a misconfigured Azure storage blob containing 120 GB of sensitive data, including names, emails, and donation details of 55,000 Ukrainian military donors. The public access setting allowed anyone to download the data.
process_and_outcome: Identified by cybersecurity researchers, the group secured the storage promptly after notification. No fines issued as it was a non-profit; led to broader awareness of cloud misconfigurations.

example_background: In October 2016, hackers accessed Uber's GitHub repository using compromised credentials, downloading data of 57 million users and drivers, including names, emails, and phone numbers. Uber paid the hackers $100,000 to delete the data and did not disclose the incident promptly.
process_and_outcome: The FTC and other regulators investigated, resulting in enhanced privacy commitments. Uber improved security and reporting processes. No monetary penalty from FTC but ongoing monitoring.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children’s Data Transfers Investigation
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating the TikTok platform in the EEA, processed personal data of users, including minors, and transferred such data to servers and entities in China and other locations outside the EEA. The company utilized various transfer mechanisms, but regulators scrutinized whether these complied with GDPR requirements for international data transfers, particularly in light of national security laws in China potentially affecting data protection guarantees. The investigation focused on data of children under 16, examining default privacy settings, age verification, and safeguards for cross-border flows.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a decision in 2023 fining TikTok €345 million for violations related to children’s data processing and transfers. Separate ongoing inquiries addressed international transfer compliance, with cross-EU cooperation via the EDPB. TikTok implemented changes to settings and transfer practices.

example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, part of Meta, transferred EEA user personal data, including chat metadata and account information, to other Meta entities in the US for service provision and analytics. Post-Schrems II, the company relied on updated SCCs, but regulators assessed whether additional measures were sufficient to mitigate risks from US surveillance laws, given the volume and sensitivity of the data involved in end-to-end encrypted communications.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC, with input from other EU authorities via the EDPB, fined WhatsApp €225 million in 2021, partly for transfer issues. Further proceedings addressed ongoing compliance, leading to mandated adjustments in transfer practices.

example_background: Oracle Corporation, via its Irish entity, offered cloud services processing EEA customer data with potential transfers to the US. An investigation examined the use of SCCs and whether technical measures like encryption and audit rights adequately addressed risks from foreign laws post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg CNPD fined Oracle €15,000 in 2023 for insufficient safeguards in data transfers. Oracle was directed to enhance measures and faced ongoing monitoring.
involved_parties: Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD)

example_background: Google Ireland provided Google Analytics and similar tools to EEA website operators, resulting in IP addresses and user data being sent to Google LLC in the US for processing. Multiple national DPAs, including in France and Austria, investigated whether consent banners and SCCs provided sufficient basis and safeguards for these transfers under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: Authorities in Austria, France, and Italy issued orders to suspend transfers in 2022-2023, citing inadequate protections. Google updated tools with IP anonymization and EEA storage options, resolving some disputes without fines.

example_name: Google LLC GDPR Fine - Location Data
example_background: The French data protection authority, CNIL, investigated Google’s Android operating system and associated services for processing personal data related to user location and advertising. The inquiry focused on the lawfulness of data processing bases, transparency in privacy policies, and adequacy of user consent mechanisms. Google's centralized privacy policy covering multiple services was found lacking in specificity about data combinations across apps like YouTube, Google Search, and Maps. Location history features continued collecting data even when users believed they had disabled tracking. The case highlighted challenges in consent granularity for ecosystems with interconnected services and the responsibilities of OS providers for pre-installed apps.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine in 2019, one of the first major GDPR penalties. Google appealed but ultimately enhanced its consent flows, introducing more granular controls and separate consents for personalized ads. The decision influenced similar enforcement across EU member states.
involved_parties: French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_background: Microsoft established a global privacy program featuring 3,000 dedicated privacy professionals and invested $20 billion in cybersecurity over five years. The company developed tools like Microsoft Purview for data governance and implemented Privacy by Design across Azure cloud services. Microsoft pioneered EU Data Boundary protections, ensuring public sector customers could keep data in EU-controlled environments. Features like differential privacy for analytics and confidential computing protected data during processing. The company publishes annual transparency reports detailing government data requests and has never been fined under GDPR despite handling petabytes of enterprise data.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft earned trust through voluntary compliance audits, ISO 27701 certification, and leadership in privacy-enhancing technologies. The program supported regulatory approvals for cloud services used by governments and achieved HITRUST certification for healthcare data handling.

example_name: Schrems II CJEU Judgment on Facebook Transfers
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited transferred personal data of over 400 million EU users to the US for processing on Facebook, Inc. servers. The transfers relied on the 2010 version of Standard Contractual Clauses, challenged by Max Schrems due to discrepancies between EU data protection standards and US surveillance practices, including bulk collection under FISA 702 and EO 12333 without equivalent safeguards.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) in Case C-311/18 Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and declared the 2010 SCCs invalid in the context of US transfers lacking supplementary protections. The court required case-by-case assessments and effective supplementary measures. Facebook suspended new transfers and deleted certain EU data stored in the US, leading to subsequent reliance on 2021 SCCs.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Clearview AI Global Data Transfers Fine
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition company, scraped billions of facial images from public websites worldwide, including EU sources, and provided access to this biometric database to clients globally. Data of EU residents was transferred to US servers without appropriate safeguards, bypassing consent and relying inadequately on contractual terms amid Schrems II implications.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU regulators investigated; the Dutch DPA fined Clearview €30.5 million in September 2022 for unlawful data processing and transfers. The Dutch court upheld the fine in 2024. Similar actions by French CNIL (€20 million fine) and others followed, prohibiting further processing.

example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) offers cloud computing services to EU customers, involving personal data transfers to regions including the US. AWS established a comprehensive Data Processing Addendum incorporating the 2021 SCCs, along with supplementary measures such as data localization options in EU regions and encryption at rest and in transit.
process_and_outcome: AWS voluntarily updated its agreements ahead of the 2021 SCC enforcement deadline, undergoing audits and providing customer guidance. EU regulators acknowledged the framework during inquiries, with no enforcement actions against AWS for transfer mechanisms as of 2024.

example_background: Google Cloud Platform facilitates data processing for EU enterprises, with transfers to US data centers for global services. Post-Schrems II, Google integrated the modular 2021 SCCs into its Data Processing Terms, offering customers choices for data residency and supplementary protections against third-country access.
process_and_outcome: Google completed migration to 2021 SCCs by the deadline, publishing detailed documentation and tools for compliance verification. It resolved related DPC inquiries without penalties, positioning as a compliant provider.

example_background: Oracle provides cloud services with EU data centers but supports hybrid models involving US processing. Oracle updated its cloud contracts to embed the 2021 SCCs, including annexes detailing transfer flows, sub-processor lists, and audit rights for customers.
process_and_outcome: Following EDPB guidance, Oracle's implementation passed regulatory reviews, with no adverse findings in GDPR audits related to transfers.

example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, part of Meta, transfers chat metadata and user data from EU to US entities for service operations. After adopting 2021 SCCs, it was investigated for adequacy of supplementary measures given US laws.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC's 2023 decision found transfers compliant under 2021 SCCs with supplements, unlike Facebook's. No fine imposed; decision appealed but upheld compliance framework.

example_name: Max Schrems Complaint Against Google Transfers
example_background: noyb filed complaints against Google for EU-US data transfers in advertising and analytics services, questioning SCC effectiveness post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing investigations by French CNIL and others as of 2024; preliminary findings noted need for enhanced supplements but no final fines yet.

example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating in the EU through its Irish subsidiary, underwent scrutiny by the Irish Data Protection Commission concerning its processing of children's personal data. The platform's age verification processes allowed users under 13 to create accounts, leading to the processing of data from minors without parental consent. Additional concerns involved default public settings for user-generated content, profiling for algorithmic recommendations, and data transfers outside the EEA. The inquiry examined how biometric data from videos and precise location information were processed for content moderation and personalization.
process_and_outcome: In 2023 and 2024, the Irish DPC fined TikTok 345 million euros for data processing violations affecting children and an additional 530 million euros for unlawful transfers. TikTok agreed to audits and operational changes, with appeals filed.

example_background: Microsoft developed a robust privacy program integrated into its products and services, emphasizing data minimization, user controls, and compliance with global data protection laws. Initiatives include the Microsoft Privacy Principles, which guide data handling across Azure cloud services, Office 365, and Windows. Features like Intelligent Security Graph anonymize telemetry data, while GDPR-specific tools enable data subject requests, data export, and deletion. Microsoft established data residency commitments, ensuring customer data stays within designated regions, and invests in privacy-preserving technologies such as homomorphic encryption.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved GDPR readiness certification pre-2018 and maintains a clean enforcement record with EU regulators. In 2023, it published its Global Privacy Report detailing compliance metrics and has supported thousands of data subject requests annually.

example_background: Salesforce maintains a publicly available list of subprocessors used for processing customer data in its cloud services, including entities like Amazon Web Services for hosting, Google for analytics, and Zendesk for support. The company updates this list regularly and notifies customers of changes, allowing opt-out options for certain subprocessors. This practice stems from commitments in customer data processing agreements to ensure transparency in the supply chain.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Salesforce's approach has been recognized in industry reports as a model for compliance, contributing to trust and avoiding regulatory scrutiny in data protection audits.

example_background: In 2022, the Irish Data Protection Commission investigated Didi Global for processing EU users' personal data using subprocessors in China without adequate safeguards or customer notifications. The ride-hailing company's app collected location, payment, and biometric data, which was routed through undisclosed third-party processors, raising concerns over data transfers outside the EU and lack of transparency in the processing chain.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued a decision highlighting non-compliance, leading to operational restrictions in Europe and a temporary app suspension. Didi committed to remediation, including subprocessor audits.

example_background: Microsoft publishes a detailed subprocessor list for Azure cloud services, covering over 100 entities like Akamai for CDN, Fastly for edge computing, and third-party data centers. Customers are informed via the Data Protection Addendum, with options to object to new subprocessors. This setup supports global data processing while maintaining oversight through audits and certifications.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft has successfully passed numerous GDPR adequacy decisions and SOC 2 audits, demonstrating effective subprocessor management without incurring fines related to this area.

example_name: Google Cloud Platform Subprocessor List Compliance
example_background: Google Cloud provides a comprehensive, searchable list of subprocessors for its services, including entities like Google subsidiaries for storage, Equinix for data centers, and Zscaler for security. Changes are announced 30 days in advance, with customer approval processes integrated into agreements, ensuring controlled data flows across global infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: Google has maintained compliance through EU Data Protection Board approvals and zero fines related to subprocessor transparency, bolstering its position in cloud markets.

example_background: In 2020, the Dutch Data Protection Authority examined TikTok's use of Chinese-based subprocessors for processing European user data, including behavioral analytics and content moderation. Concerns arose over inadequate contractual protections and transparency regarding data handling by these third parties, amid broader scrutiny of cross-border data flows.
process_and_outcome: The investigation resulted in orders for TikTok to adjust processing activities, contributing to a temporary EU-wide ban threat lifted after compliance undertakings.

example_background: AWS discloses its subprocessor list in customer agreements, listing affiliates and third parties like Snowhite for backups and Iron Mountain for archiving. Customers receive notifications of additions, with veto rights, supporting compliant data processing in multi-tenant environments worldwide.
process_and_outcome: AWS achieved continuous GDPR certification and no subprocessor-related penalties, serving as a benchmark in cloud compliance reports.

example_background: OpenAI maintains a publicly accessible list of sub-processors used in its platform services, detailing entities involved in data processing activities such as cloud hosting, analytics, and support functions. This list is hosted at https://platform.openai.com/subprocessors and includes companies like AWS, Google Cloud, and others, with details on their locations and purposes. OpenAI commits to notifying customers of any new sub-processors or changes via email or in-app notices, allowing customers to object within a specified period. This practice supports data processing agreements under various privacy frameworks by providing visibility into the supply chain of data handlers.
process_and_outcome: OpenAI regularly updates the list to reflect current sub-processors and has implemented a process for customer notifications and objections, resulting in sustained compliance with contractual obligations and no reported disruptions from sub-processor changes.

example_background: Salesforce, a major cloud service provider, publishes a detailed sub-processor list on its trust site, covering over 50 entities involved in hosting, logging, and security scanning for its CRM platform. The list specifies each sub-processor's location, services provided, and links to their privacy policies. Salesforce requires customer consent for new sub-processors and provides options for restrictions in enterprise agreements. This approach has been in place since 2016 and is audited annually as part of SOC 2 compliance efforts.
process_and_outcome: Independent audits confirm the accuracy of the list, and customer feedback has led to refinements, maintaining high trust levels with enterprise clients across industries.

example_background: Google Cloud Platform provides a comprehensive sub-processor list for its services, including G Suite and Cloud Identity, listing entities like Twilio for telephony and various regional data centers. The list is updated quarterly and accessible via the Google Cloud compliance resource center. Customers are informed of changes 30 days in advance, with options to terminate services if objections arise. This transparency supports Google's role as a processor for thousands of organizations handling sensitive data.
process_and_outcome: Google's disclosures have facilitated GDPR compliance for clients, with no major enforcement actions related to sub-processor transparency reported.

example_background: Slack Technologies publishes its sub-processor list covering services like Amazon Web Services for hosting, Stripe for billing, and Intercom for support. The list is available in Slack's security and compliance documentation and updated as partnerships change. Slack notifies enterprise customers of additions or substitutions, aligning with its status page and trust center updates. This practice ensures customers understand data flow across third parties.
process_and_outcome: Following acquisition by Salesforce, Slack integrated its list into broader compliance frameworks, enhancing visibility and avoiding service interruptions.

example_background: AWS maintains a sub-processors page detailing affiliates and third parties involved in service delivery, such as data center operators and security providers. Customers can access lists specific to services like S3 or EC2. AWS commits not to use sub-processors without customer authorization in certain regions and provides transparency reports. This system supports AWS's handling of petabytes of customer data daily.
process_and_outcome: AWS's approach has withstood multiple regulatory audits, contributing to its leadership in cloud compliance certifications.

example_background: Meta (Facebook) provides sub-processor lists for services like WhatsApp Business API and Facebook Ads, including entities for cloud storage and analytics. Following GDPR enforcement, Meta enhanced its disclosures with customer notification mechanisms and objection rights. The lists are part of enterprise agreements and updated via legal notices.
process_and_outcome: Post-2018 GDPR investigations, Meta improved its practices, reducing complaints related to third-party data handling.

example_background: In 2014, Dropbox experienced unauthorized access by third-party contractors in China who were not clearly listed as sub-processors in customer agreements. Reports indicated that data from thousands of accounts was reviewed without explicit customer notification or consent mechanisms. Dropbox's processor agreements at the time lacked a public list, leading to questions about data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: Dropbox conducted an internal review, terminated the contractors, and updated its transparency policies by publishing a sub-processor list. No regulatory fines were imposed, but the incident prompted policy enhancements.

example_background: Microsoft faced scrutiny in 2020 when enterprise customers reported lack of timely notice for new sub-processors added to Azure services, including changes involving regional partners. Internal audits revealed delays in updating the public list and notifying customers under DPA terms.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft accelerated its notification processes and expanded list details, resolving customer concerns without formal enforcement.

example_background: Twilio, a communications platform, was involved in a 2022 customer dispute where changes to its sub-processor list for SMS services were not adequately notified to all users. The list, hosted on Twilio's trust site, added new analytics providers without the standard 30-day notice to smaller customers.
process_and_outcome: Twilio settled with affected customers through service credits and improved its notification system, updating the list with more granular details.

example_name: TikTok UK GDPR Data Transfer Investigation
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, faced scrutiny over its handling of UK user data, particularly concerning transfers to China following Brexit. The platform, with millions of UK users primarily under 30, collected extensive personal data including location, device information, and behavioral data. Post-Brexit, reliance on EU Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for international transfers raised questions about adequacy, leading to concerns that data protections were not sufficiently maintained outside the UK. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined whether TikTok's transfer mechanisms aligned with UK data protection requirements, amid broader EU investigations into similar practices.
process_and_outcome: The ICO launched an investigation in 2021, resulting in a preliminary enforcement notice in 2023 that required TikTok to halt transfers of UK users' data to China until compliance was assured. TikTok committed to implementing UK-specific safeguards, including the UK Addendum to SCCs, and faced ongoing monitoring.

example_name: Microsoft Adopts UK IDTA for Azure Services
example_background: Microsoft updated its Azure cloud services contracts for UK customers to incorporate the UK's International Data Transfer Agreement (IDTA) and the UK Addendum to EU SCCs following the adequacy decision's expiration. This ensured that personal data processed through Azure data centers and transferred to the US or other locations maintained equivalent protections under UK GDPR. The change was part of a broader compliance strategy affecting enterprise customers in finance, healthcare, and public sectors who rely on cloud infrastructure for sensitive data handling.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft proactively issued new Data Protection Addendum terms in 2022, enabling seamless compliance for UK clients without service disruptions. No enforcement actions were taken, and the company received positive feedback from regulators for transparency.

example_name: Clearview AI UK Data Scraping Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI, a facial recognition company, scraped billions of images from UK websites without consent and stored them in a US-based database accessible globally. UK individuals' biometric data was transferred overseas without appropriate safeguards, prompting complaints about unlawful processing and transfers. The company's business model involved selling access to law enforcement and private entities, raising significant privacy concerns in the post-Brexit landscape where UK-specific transfer rules applied.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty of £7.5 million in 2022 and a enforcement notice prohibiting further unlawful transfers. Clearview AI was found non-compliant and ordered to delete UK data, with appeals pending.

example_name: Google Cloud UK Transfer Compliance Update
example_background: Google Cloud Platform revised its model clauses for UK and Swiss customers to include the UK Addendum and IDTA, addressing data transfers to the US amid Schrems II invalidation effects. This impacted thousands of UK organizations using GCP for SaaS applications, AI services, and data analytics, ensuring continued lawful transfers of personal data like customer records and employee information across borders.
process_and_outcome: In March 2022, Google rolled out updated terms, providing customers with compliant clauses and supplementary measures like encryption and pseudonymization. The ICO acknowledged the updates as aligning with guidance, avoiding any penalties.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Transfer Issues
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a cyber-attack exposing 400,000 customers' personal and payment data, some of which was transferred to third-party processors in non-adequate countries without updated safeguards. Post-Brexit audits revealed reliance on outdated EU SCCs without the UK Addendum, complicating compliance during the ICO investigation into the breach response and international data flows.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined British Airways £20 million in 2020 (reduced from £22 million), citing failures in securing and transferring data. The airline implemented new transfer mechanisms including the UK Addendum as part of remediation.

example_name: Salesforce UK Data Transfer Addendum Adoption
example_background: Salesforce, a major CRM provider, integrated the UK Addendum into its Data Processing Addendum for UK customers using services like Sales Cloud and Service Cloud. This covered transfers of customer relationship data to US data centers, incorporating technical measures such as data localization options and access controls to meet UK adequacy requirements post-Brexit.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce updated contracts in early 2021, with no reported ICO actions. Customers transitioned smoothly, and the company published guidance on compliant transfers, demonstrating proactive alignment.

example_name: ICO Enforcement Against Data Broker Transatlantic Transfers
example_background: A UK-based data broker transferred personal data of millions to US affiliates without implementing required safeguards after Brexit. The data included consumer profiles used for marketing, compiled from public and private sources, and moved via cloud services without the UK Addendum or equivalent protections.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the ICO issued a £2.9 million fine and enforcement notice, requiring cessation of non-compliant transfers and data deletion. The broker entered into an undertaking to adopt proper mechanisms.

example_name: Oracle Cloud UK SCC Addendum Implementation
example_background: Oracle updated its cloud service agreements to include the UK Addendum for transfers from UK regions to its global infrastructure, particularly for Fusion Applications handling HR and financial data. This ensured compliance for public sector and financial services clients subject to strict data localization preferences.
process_and_outcome: Implemented in 2022 with automated clause insertion in contracts. Oracle conducted audits and provided certification, aligning with ICO guidance without incidents.

example_background: Meta (Facebook) was investigated for transferring UK user data to the US for advertising and analytics purposes using standard clauses invalidated by Schrems II. Post-Brexit, the lack of UK Addendum implementation persisted, affecting data from Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp with over 40 million UK users.
process_and_outcome: The ICO coordinated with EU regulators, contributing to a 2023 Irish DPC decision fining Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful transfers. Meta was ordered to suspend transfers and adopt compliant UK mechanisms.

example_name: TikTok EU Data Transfers DPA Violations
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, engaged third-party processors for handling European user data, including transfers outside the EU to servers and services in China and other locations. The company updated its terms multiple times during investigations, but regulators found ongoing issues with data processing by sub-processors without sufficient safeguards or notifications to data controllers. This involved processing of personal data from millions of users for content recommendation and advertising purposes, raising concerns over the necessity and duration of such processing.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an investigation and issued a decision fining TikTok €345 million in 2023 for violations related to children's data and sub-processor arrangements. Additional scrutiny led to cross-border enforcement by other EU regulators.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to Third Countries)

example_name: Meta Platforms Schrems II Sub-Processor Transfers
example_background: Meta Platforms transferred European user data to sub-processors in the US for services like data hosting and analytics. Following the Schrems II ruling invalidating Privacy Shield, Meta relied on Standard Contractual Clauses but faced challenges with unauthorized access risks by US authorities. Sub-processors included AWS and other cloud providers, processing data necessary for platform operations over extended periods.
process_and_outcome: Multiple investigations by EU regulators resulted in injunctions and a €1.2 billion fine by the Irish DPC in 2023, requiring Meta to suspend transfers and implement supplementary measures.

example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) acted as a data processor for EU customers, appointing sub-processors for cloud infrastructure services including data storage and computation. An investigation revealed that AWS did not properly inform controllers about all sub-processor identities and locations, particularly for global data centers, with processing continuing for the service duration without explicit written agreements on limitations.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD fined AWS €746 million in 2021, one of the largest GDPR penalties, following complaints about transparency and control over sub-processors.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD)

example_background: Google, as a processor for advertising and cloud services, used numerous sub-processors worldwide for handling customer data in products like Google Cloud and Ads. Regulators identified failures in maintaining up-to-date lists of sub-processors and obtaining customer consents for changes, with data processing extending across service agreements without clear boundaries.
process_and_outcome: French SA fined Google €150 million in 2022 for cookie consent violations, with linked findings on sub-processor management; separate ongoing EU probes into cloud services.

example_background: Microsoft Azure provided cloud services to EU customers, utilizing sub-processors in the US and elsewhere for data processing tasks essential to service delivery, such as backups and analytics. Post-Schrems II, Microsoft updated its DPA to include sub-processor lists and commitments to notify customers of changes, ensuring processing aligned with agreement durations.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft implemented Data Protection Addendum (DPA) enhancements and EU Data Boundary solutions, avoiding major fines and demonstrating compliance through transparency reports and audits.

example_background: Salesforce, a CRM platform provider, maintains a public list of sub-processors used for services like data hosting and security monitoring. Customers can object to specific sub-processors, and Salesforce ensures all processing is limited to service performance with notifications for any changes, covering the full term of customer agreements.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce has not faced significant sub-processor fines in EU probes and publishes regular transparency reports, serving as an industry benchmark for compliance.

example_name: Clearview AI Sub-Processor and Transfer Violations
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images and used US-based sub-processors for storage and processing, transferring data from EU subjects without adequate safeguards or controller agreements. Processing continued indefinitely beyond any service necessity, leading to complaints from privacy advocates.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU fines totaling over €30 million from authorities in France, Greece, Italy, and others; banned from processing EU data in several jurisdictions.

example_background: Snowflake, a cloud data platform, experienced a 2024 breach affecting customers like Ticketmaster and Santander. Attackers exploited lack of multi-factor authentication, but investigations highlighted sub-processor configurations for customer data access that allowed unauthorized processing without proper oversight during service use.
process_and_outcome: Joint investigations by cybersecurity firms and regulators; Snowflake enhanced sub-processor controls and MFA mandates, with affected parties notifying millions of users.

example_background: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure provides services with a detailed DPA specifying sub-processors for regions like EU Frankfurt, limiting processing to service delivery needs and providing customers with veto rights and audit capabilities throughout the agreement term.
process_and_outcome: Oracle maintains GDPR compliance certification and has not incurred major sub-processor fines, using model clauses for transparency.

example_name: OpenAI Data Processing Agreement Enforcement - Reddit Data Scraping
example_background: OpenAI utilized data obtained from Reddit through automated scraping processes to train its language models. This involved collecting user-generated content from Reddit's public forums without explicit individual consents for such training purposes. The data processing occurred as part of OpenAI's service development under its general agreements, extending beyond the specified term durations outlined in its data processing commitments. Reddit entered into a licensing agreement with OpenAI after the fact, highlighting the initial processing nature tied to model training services.
process_and_outcome: Reddit disclosed the data licensing deal in its S-1 filing with the SEC, valued at $50 million initially with potential for $200 million over two years. OpenAI integrated Reddit content into models like GPT, with ongoing data access provisions.

example_background: Google processed European user data via Analytics tools for website service performance under agreements with numerous controllers. Data transfers to the U.S. occurred without sufficient safeguards, with processing durations extending beyond service terms due to aggregated retention practices not aligned with deletion obligations post-term.
process_and_outcome: Multiple European regulators, including the French CNIL, banned Google Analytics transfers. CNIL issued formal notices and enforcement actions against non-compliant sites.

example_name: Meta (Facebook) Cambridge Analytica Data Processing Breach
example_background: Meta processed data of 87 million users via third-party apps for political advertising services, extending processing beyond app service terms into indefinite retention periods without deletion post-term. The nature shifted from core platform services to unauthorized profiling.
process_and_outcome: FTC imposed $5 billion penalty. UK ICO fined £500,000. Multiple class actions and regulatory settlements followed.

example_name: Salesforce Data Processing Addendum Compliance Model
example_background: Salesforce implemented a comprehensive DPA for its CRM services, detailing processing nature limited to customer relationship management under agreements, with durations tied to subscription terms plus deletion periods. Transfers use EU Standard Contractual Clauses with supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: Successfully navigated GDPR audits and Schrems II requirements, maintaining trust with enterprise customers through annual compliance validations.

example_name: Amazon Web Services GDPR DPA Enforcement - Italy Fine
example_background: AWS processed data for Italian clients under cloud service agreements, but configurations led to transfers outside EU without adequate protections, with retention exceeding post-term deletion requirements in some instances.
process_and_outcome: Italian DPA fined AWS €6.4 million for issues in customer data export configurations violating transfer rules.
involved_parties: Italian Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_background: Oracle's cloud services processed EU customer data for database management under service contracts, involving U.S. transfers and retention practices that extended beyond specified agreement terms without compliant deletion protocols.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL fined Oracle €10 million for unlawful data transfers to the U.S. and lack of transparency in processing activities.

example_background: CMT Digital, LLC, doing business as Coldwater IT, operated websites that hosted advertisements and collected personal information from California residents through cookies, pixels, and other tracking technologies. The company sold or shared this data, including browsing behavior and IP addresses, with third-party advertisers without implementing a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link or providing clear opt-out mechanisms. Privacy notices on the websites were insufficient, failing to inform consumers about the categories of data collected and shared. This affected numerous visitors to the sites over multiple years.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) issued its first enforcement action in 2024, fining CMT Digital $1.2 million. The company was required to cease unauthorized data sales and implement compliance measures including updated privacy policies and opt-out functionalities.

example_background: IKEA, the furniture retailer, utilized tracking tools on its U.S. websites and apps to collect personal information from California consumers, including identifiers, geolocation data, and inferences about purchasing preferences. This data was shared with third-party vendors for advertising without prominent disclosures or an accessible opt-out link for the sale or sharing of personal information. The company's privacy policy did not adequately describe these practices, and consumers were not informed of their rights to know, delete, or opt out of such data handling.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, IKEA settled with the California Attorney General, paying $300,000 in penalties. The agreement mandated improvements to privacy notices, addition of opt-out mechanisms, annual privacy audits, and employee training for three years.

example_background: BetterHelp, an online therapy service, gathered sensitive mental health data from users through quizzes, session notes, and intake forms. The company shared this personal information, including health conditions and therapy topics, with advertising platforms like Facebook and Snapchat for targeting purposes without obtaining user consent. Privacy policies stated that data would not be shared for advertising, but the practices contradicted these assurances, affecting over a million consumers.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the FTC settled with BetterHelp, requiring a $7.8 million monetary judgment (partially suspended) and prohibitions on sharing sensitive health data for advertising without explicit consent. The company had to notify affected users and establish a privacy program with regular assessments.

example_background: Vroom, an online used car retailer, collected personal data from California consumers via its website, including names, addresses, phone numbers, and financial information for vehicle purchases. The company sold or shared this data with third parties without clear disclosures or an operational 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link, impeding consumers' ability to exercise CCPA rights.
process_and_outcome: The California Attorney General reached a settlement in 2022 with Vroom, which included $100,000 in penalties. Vroom agreed to enhance privacy disclosures, enable opt-out rights, and conduct privacy training and audits for three years.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fines for User Data
example_background: Meta Platforms, operating Facebook, faced multiple enforcement actions in the EU for mishandling user data. In 2023, Ireland's Data Protection Commission fined Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers of European users' personal information to the U.S. following the Schrems II ruling. Affected data subjects included end users—hundreds of millions of Facebook users—who shared profiles, posts, contacts, and location data through the platform for social networking. Transfers relied on invalid Privacy Shield mechanisms post-2020 court invalidation.
process_and_outcome: The fine was upheld after appeals. Meta was required to suspend transatlantic data transfers, implement new Standard Contractual Clauses with supplementary measures, and face ongoing audits. Additional fines for other violations totaled over €2 billion.

example_name: Uber Data Breach Involving Drivers and Riders
example_background: Uber disclosed a 2016 data breach in 2017, where hackers accessed data on 57 million riders and drivers worldwide. Rider data included names, emails, and phone numbers; driver data encompassed license numbers and bank details. Data subjects were end users (riders booking trips) and suppliers (drivers providing services via the app). Uber paid hackers $100,000 to delete data and concealed the incident for a year.
process_and_outcome: FTC imposed a $148 million penalty: $20 million U.S. settlement and $128 million to drivers across states. Uber enhanced security with bug bounties and transparency reporting.

example_background: In 2022, cybersecurity researchers discovered misconfigured Microsoft Azure cloud storage buckets exposing sensitive data from multiple organizations, including passports, fingerprints, and vaccination certificates. Microsoft customers transferred highly sensitive data to cloud environments without fully implementing access restrictions, encryption, or logging as recommended. The incidents highlighted gaps in safeguards for data in transit and at rest within Microsoft's ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft investigated and secured the buckets, issuing guidance on secure configurations. No direct fines were imposed on Microsoft, but it led to enhanced tools for customers and internal audits.

example_background: In 2022, Salesforce faced scrutiny after reports revealed that sensitive customer data from major brands like Volkswagen and Santander was accessible via unsecured Salesforce instances. Data including contracts, invoices, and personal details was transferable without authentication controls or purpose limitations, due to misconfigurations in customer implementations lacking training and access logs.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce notified affected parties and provided remediation tools. No major regulatory fines were reported, but it prompted industry-wide reviews of SaaS security practices.

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp Transfers Sanctioned by French CNIL
example_background: The French data protection authority, CNIL, investigated WhatsApp's data transfer practices from Europe to the US following the Schrems II ruling. WhatsApp, a Meta company, continued using the invalidated Privacy Shield for several months post-ruling and relied on previous versions of SCCs without implementing supplementary measures to address risks from US surveillance laws. The inquiry focused on transparency in privacy policies and the effectiveness of safeguards for categories of data such as usage analytics and account information transferred for service provision and advertising purposes.
process_and_outcome: In July 2021, CNIL imposed a 50 million euro fine on WhatsApp for non-compliance with GDPR transfer rules. WhatsApp updated its practices to adopt new SCCs and supplementary measures.

example_name: Salesforce EU-US SCC Implementation Post-Schrems II
example_background: Salesforce, a cloud services provider, processes customer data as a processor for EU clients, involving transfers to the US for hosting and support services. Following the Schrems II decision, Salesforce conducted a thorough transfer impact assessment and supplemented its processor agreements with technical measures such as encryption at rest and in transit, pseudonymization, and organizational safeguards to prevent unauthorized access, including by government entities.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce published its Data Transfer Impact Assessment (DTIA) and adopted the 2021 SCCs with addendums. No enforcement actions were taken; EU supervisory authorities acknowledged the measures as sufficient.

example_name: Google Cloud EU Data Processing Addendum
example_background: Google Cloud offers services under DPA terms specifying the use of SCCs for data transfers from the EU/UK to the US where customer data is processed on behalf of controllers. The DPA delineates roles with Google acting as processor or sub-processor, incorporating Module Two for controller-processor scenarios and Module Three for processor-sub-processor flows. Additional protections include data localization options, encryption, and audit rights.
process_and_outcome: Google's framework has been reviewed in multiple adequacy decisions and binding corporate rules validations, with no major transfer-related fines issued by EU regulators.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Standard Contractual Clauses (Modules Two and Three)

example_name: Amazon Web Services (AWS) GDPR Compliance Certification
example_background: AWS provides cloud infrastructure services where it processes customer data as a processor, often transferring it to data centers in the US. AWS's Data Processing Addendum (DPA) explicitly incorporates the 2021 SCCs, specifying Module Two for controller-to-processor transfers and Module Three where AWS uses sub-processors. Customers can select regions to minimize transfers, and AWS provides tools for impact assessments.
process_and_outcome: AWS received positive assessments from EU regulators and maintained ISO certifications. No transfer-specific enforcement actions recorded.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Standard Contractual Clauses (Modules Two and Three)

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into Transfers to India by Fortune 500 Firm
example_background: A major technology company headquartered in the US used SCCs for transferring HR and customer data to shared service centers in India, a non-adequate jurisdiction. The Irish DPC probe followed Schrems II, scrutinizing the effectiveness of SCCs given potential government access under Indian laws, and the lack of supplementary measures like encryption or audit logs.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the DPC found gaps in safeguards and issued corrective orders. The company implemented additional protections to resume compliant transfers.

example_name: Microsoft Azure UK GDPR SCC Adoption
example_background: Microsoft Azure, as a processor for UK customers, updated its agreements to include the UK International Data Transfer Addendum (IDTA) alongside EU SCCs for transfers involving UK personal data. This covers scenarios where Microsoft processes data as processor (Module Two) or engages sub-processors (Module Three), with commitments to notify of sub-processor use and maintain security certifications.
process_and_outcome: Validated through UK ICO's international transfer guidance; Microsoft achieved compliance without sanctions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Standard Contractual Clauses Module Two and Three

example_background: OpenAI experienced an incident with ChatGPT in March 2023 where a bug in the Redis open-source library caused some users' chat history titles and content to become visible to others. The issue affected a limited number of users, exposing conversation data that included personal information shared in prompts. ChatGPT processes user inputs, which may contain sensitive data if users include it unexpectedly, and this bug led to unintended visibility of such unstructured data across accounts for about nine hours.
process_and_outcome: OpenAI identified and fixed the bug, notified affected users, and conducted a review of safeguards. No evidence of widespread data misuse was found, and the company enhanced library configurations to prevent recurrence.

example_background: In 2023, Microsoft's 365 admin center displayed email content and attachments from other tenants due to a caching issue during failover events. Sensitive business email data from unintended organizations was briefly accessible to unauthorized administrators. The platform handles structured email data across tenants, and the glitch allowed cross-tenant data visibility for a short period.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft investigated, resolved the issue, and implemented mitigations. Affected customers were notified, and no data was exfiltrated externally according to reports.

example_background: In 2014, celebrities' private photos stored in iCloud were accessed and disseminated online. Attackers used phishing to obtain Apple IDs and exploited iCloud's Find My iPhone feature to reset passwords, accessing photo backups. The incident involved sensitive media data uploaded by users to iCloud for storage and synchronization across devices.
process_and_outcome: Apple enhanced security with two-factor authentication mandates and improved phishing detection. Investigations by the FBI traced some perpetrators, leading to arrests.

example_background: TikTok, owned by ByteDance, faced scrutiny for its handling of children's data under its musical.ly app predecessor and continued practices. In 2019, the app was found to collect data from users under 13 without proper parental consent, with data being transferred to Chinese servers on a continuous basis as users interacted with the platform. Location data, device identifiers, and behavioral data were sent in real-time streams during video watching, liking, and sharing activities. This ongoing transfer occurred without distinguishing frequency controls for minors, leading to millions of profiles being built over time. The FTC investigation revealed that even after COPPA compliance promises, data continued to flow regularly to parent company servers.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with TikTok for $5.7 million in 2019, the largest COPPA fine at the time. TikTok was required to implement age-gating, data deletion processes, and restrict data transfers for children's accounts. Further investigations by state AGs and the EU led to additional privacy program overhauls.

example_background: CNIL, the French data protection authority, investigated Google's use of Google Analytics on websites in 2022. The tool involves continuous transfer of EU users' IP addresses and browsing data to US servers every time a page loads, creating real-time data streams for analytics and advertising. Despite Google's claims of pseudonymization, CNIL determined these transfers lacked adequate safeguards under post-Schrems II standards, as data flowed repeatedly without sufficient localization or consent mechanisms distinguishing one-off from ongoing transfers. Similar probes occurred in Austria, Italy, and elsewhere, highlighting the frequency aspect in transatlantic data flows.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google €150 million in 2022 and ordered cessation of unlawful transfers. Google appealed but implemented EU data residency options. Other EU regulators issued comparable decisions, leading to widespread suspension of Google Analytics on EU sites.

example_name: Meta Transfers to US Servers (Irish DPC)
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) examined Meta's (Facebook) transfers of EU user data to the US under the now-invalid Privacy Shield. From 2020-2023, vast amounts of personal data—including posts, messages, and profile info—were transferred continuously to US data centers for processing, storage, and ad targeting. Every user interaction triggered ongoing data flows, with no mechanisms to limit frequency post-Schrems II. Meta relied on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) but failed to implement supplementary measures for continuous high-volume transfers, affecting hundreds of millions of users.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion, the largest GDPR fine to date, and ordered suspension of transatlantic transfers. Meta appealed to the CJEU and shifted some data processing to EU servers. Enforcement actions spread across EU DPAs.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_background: Unilever updated its GDPR-compliant Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with vendors in 2018-2020, explicitly categorizing data transfers by frequency: one-off for batch exports (e.g., annual audits) versus continuous for real-time analytics (e.g., CRM syncing). Schedules in DPAs detailed transfer types, volumes, and safeguards like encryption for ongoing flows. This allowed processors to handle consumer data from marketing campaigns with clear limits, such as pseudonymization for repeated transfers and annual reviews to minimize necessity. Unilever's approach was audited positively during GDPR enforcement waves.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions taken; Unilever's practices cited in industry reports as exemplars. Passed multiple DPA audits by DPAs and third-party assessors, enabling seamless global operations without fines.

example_background: Salesforce implemented hyperforce architecture in 2021, allowing customers to choose data residency regions (e.g., EU-only servers). Customers configure transfers as one-off exports or continuous syncing with controls like field-level encryption and frequency limits (e.g., hourly batches max). For GDPR/CCPA compliance, dashboards show transfer logs by cadence, enabling clients in regulated industries like finance to restrict continuous flows to approved processors, with automatic purging after defined periods.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by thousands of enterprises without DPA fines; certified under ISO 27001 and SOC 2. Customer audits confirmed compliance, reducing breach risks in multi-tenant environments.

example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, a Meta subsidiary, was investigated for data transfers to the US involving end-to-end encrypted messages, metadata, and user analytics. The probe focused on the configuration of SCC modules, including the applicability of docking clauses, sub-processor prior notice periods, optional clauses in Clauses 11 and 13, choice of governing law (Ireland vs. others), dispute resolution forums, and completion of Annexes I, II, and III with details on security measures and supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a €225 million fine in 2021, later increased, and required WhatsApp to align SCCs with Schreiner-Cassidy II by specifying EU law, ICO-equivalent authorities, and DPA-referenced security. WhatsApp implemented changes and faced continued scrutiny.

example_name: Google Cloud SCC Modifications for GDPR
example_background: Google Cloud Platform revised its data processing addendum to incorporate Schreiner-Cassidy II-compliant SCCs for transfers from EU controllers to US processors. Configurations included no docking clause, general written authorization for sub-processors with 30-day notice, no optional Clause 11 language, filled Clause 13 brackets, Irish law governance, Irish courts for disputes, Annex I/III details in the schedule, Annex II via security section, and EU supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: Google implemented these changes in 2022-2023, passed regulatory audits by multiple EU DPAs, and avoided enforcement actions, positioning as a compliant cloud provider.

example_name: Amazon Web Services EU DPA SCC Alignment
example_background: AWS updated its EU Data Processing Addendum to embed specific SCC customizations for international transfers, addressing processor-controller and processor-sub-processor modules. This involved precise settings for Clause 7 (no docking), Clause 9 (Option 2 with notice periods), Clause 11 (no optionals), Clause 13 (brackets removed), Clause 17 (EU/UK law options), Clause 18 (UK courts), and full Annex population referencing DPA security and ICO.
process_and_outcome: Implemented in 2023, AWS received positive assessments from UK ICO and EU regulators, enabling continued operations without penalties.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Transfers Breach
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites worldwide, including EU data, and transferred them to US servers without adequate safeguards. The company used basic contracts lacking Schreiner-Cassidy II-compliant SCC modules, proper sub-processor oversight, specified governing laws, or annex details on security measures.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU DPAs, coordinated by the EDPB, fined Clearview €30.5 million total in 2022-2023 and banned processing, citing invalid transfer mechanisms.

example_background: Salesforce revised its DPA and SCCs for CRM services to meet post-Schreiner-Cassidy II standards, configuring modules with no optional docking, prior notice for sub-processors per DPA Section 2.9 equivalent, no Clause 11 optionals, completed Clause 13, UK/Irish law and courts, Annex I/III in schedule, Annex II via security section, and ICO as authority.
process_and_outcome: Updates effective 2023, validated by third-party audits and no regulatory actions, supporting customer compliance.

example_background: Oracle Cloud services faced investigation for data transfers from EU to US/India locations using outdated SCC versions without Schreiner-Cassidy II updates, including incomplete annexes and unspecified governing laws or dispute resolution.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Oracle Netherlands €10 million in 2023 for transfer violations, requiring immediate SCC revisions and flow suspensions.

example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, faced scrutiny from the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) over its transfers of European user data to China and the US. Concerns arose regarding the adequacy of protections under Chinese national intelligence laws, which could compel data disclosure to authorities, and similar issues with US surveillance practices post-Schrems II. The investigation examined TikTok's reliance on standard contractual clauses and supplementary measures amid ongoing debates on third-country adequacy. This case reflected broader tensions in global tech firms' data processing operations across jurisdictions with differing legal frameworks.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC issued a preliminary decision finding some transfers non-compliant with GDPR Chapter V, proposing fines up to 4% of global turnover. TikTok appealed, leading to escalation to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). Ongoing proceedings have resulted in operational changes to data handling practices.
involved_parties: ByteDance Technology Co. Ltd. (China/US data importer)

example_background: Following Schrems II, the Irish DPC investigated WhatsApp Ireland's data transfers to parent company entities in the US. The probe focused on whether SCCs provided sufficient safeguards against US surveillance laws, given the lack of judicial redress for EU data subjects. WhatsApp processed vast amounts of personal data including metadata from billions of users, raising questions about the effectiveness of transfer tools in ensuring data protection levels equivalent to the EU.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the DPC fined WhatsApp Ireland €1.2 billion for GDPR violations related to unlawful data transfers. The decision followed EDPB binding instructions after WhatsApp contested initial findings. WhatsApp is appealing the fine.

example_background: The Irish DPC launched an investigation into Google Ireland Limited's transfers of European personal data to Google LLC in the US. The inquiry assessed compliance with GDPR transfer requirements post-Schrems II, focusing on advertising data processing and the adequacy of safeguards against third-country access by public authorities.
process_and_outcome: Initiated in 2020, the cross-border inquiry remains ongoing as of 2024, with coordination among EU DPAs under the One-Stop-Shop mechanism. Google has implemented additional measures in response to regulatory concerns.

example_background: Oracle joined the EU-US Data Privacy Framework to support its cloud services for EU clients, specifying data exporter-importer relationships in service agreements. This ensured data processed in US regions met EU protection standards through government commitments and corporate self-certification.
process_and_outcome: Oracle self-certified in 2023, integrating DPF into customer contracts. This has supported seamless operations without regulatory challenges.

example_background: AWS, a major cloud provider, self-certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework to enable EU customers to store and process data in US regions. Agreements clearly designate AWS EMEA (exporter) and AWS US entities (importer), with commitments to verified safeguards.
process_and_outcome: Certified in August 2023, AWS updated its GDPR-compliant addendum, avoiding transfer restrictions and maintaining service continuity.
involved_parties: Amazon Web Services Inc. (US data importer)

example_name: Snowflake Joins EU-US Data Privacy Framework
example_background: Snowflake Inc., a cloud data platform, specified in its documentation EU-based account holders as data exporters and US entities as importers under the DPF. This supports multinational data warehousing while aligning with EU adequacy requirements.
process_and_outcome: Self-certification in 2023 allows compliant cross-border data flows, with no reported issues.
